BioPharmCatalyst small-molecule pricing analysis

Cumulative abnormal return (CAR, T-2..T+2 vs. XBI) around FDA Approved or CRL (Complete Response Letter) catalysts, small-molecule/peptide drugs only, filtered from the BioPharmCatalyst scrape (Tejas1415/Web-Scrape-Stock-Ticker-and-Company-Name-Datasets).

Coverage caveats, read before using these numbers: the source file covers 2009-07-05 to 2020-01-14 only — not the trailing 10 years as originally hoped; it is a static scrape, stale by ~6 years. Of 462 unique small-molecule/peptide Approved-or-CRL events (363 rows after PubChem filtering), 249 (68.6%) have priceable Yahoo Finance history — the other 114 fail because 55 tickers were delisted/acquired long enough ago that Yahoo has purged their price history entirely (e.g. ARNA, CLVS, SGEN, MYL, AGN — all real M&A exits), not because of a pipeline bug.

Headline result

CAR window is T-2..T+2 (2 trading days each side of the catalyst date), market-model-adjusted vs XBI — same estimator as the CTOD/EDGAR notebook's short_window_car(), not a longer window. See the long-window section below for the ±1yr raw-price shape.

Two-sample test (do Approved and CRL react differently from each other?): Mann-Whitney U p = 5.4e-8, Welch t-test p = 6.6e-6 — both far past conventional significance (0.05), on real sample sizes (not the n=10-83 the EDGAR-matched pipeline was stuck at). One-sample tests (does each group react differently from zero?) tell the more precise story: Approved is not significant vs. zero (Wilcoxon p=0.19) — the market treats approval as expected/priced-in, not news. CRL is highly significant vs. zero (Wilcoxon p=2.9e-6) — a CRL is the real information shock. This is the same asymmetry the CTOD/EDGAR track found for Phase 3 Success vs. Failure.

Priced events

Approved · median CAR

Mann-Whitney p (Approved vs CRL)
5.4e-8

0.19 · not sig.
CRL vs zero (Wilcoxon p)
2.9e-6 · sig.

Excluding big pharma (dropping the 18 diversified large-caps like PFE/JNJ/NVS, where one drug is a tiny fraction of firm value): the effect gets stronger, not weaker — Approved n=84 median +0.32% (vs zero p=0.95, still a non-event), CRL n=28 median −21.7% (vs zero p=6.6e-7), Approved-vs-CRL Mann-Whitney p=2.3e-8. So the signal is a small-cap phenomenon and is not being driven by the big names.

CAR by outcome

Each dot is one event, jittered horizontally. Thick tick = median. Approval is a non-event for price (already priced in); a CRL is the real information shock.

Approved (n=209)
CRL (n=40)

CAAR path: where the move actually happens

This is the "AR and CAR together" view you asked about. Each point is the cumulative average abnormal return up to that trading day; the slope between two points is that day's average abnormal return. Reading it: a line climbing steadily = the stock gains a bit almost every day (a drift); a line flat-then-jump = nothing until a one-day shock. Shaded band = the tight T-2..T+2 window used for the significance tests above.

This explains why Approved looks "not significant" in the tight window. Approved (green) drifts up gradually from about T-30 (+0.2%) to T0 (+3.1%) — only +0.7% of that lands on the event day itself. The market is pricing the approval in over the weeks before, consistent with anticipation / information leakage (your "heard the rumor" read). A 5-day window centered on T0 catches only the small tail of a move that already mostly happened, so it reads as non-significant — the move is diffuse, not absent. CRL (red) is the opposite: flat until T-1, then −10% in a single day at T0 — a genuine surprise the tight window captures fully. If you want Approved significance, widen the window to roughly T-20..T+2 (captures the run-up); but note that a wider pre-window also starts absorbing unrelated news, so treat "approval is anticipated" as the finding rather than something to engineer away.

Zooming out to 6 months refines the story. This longer path uses the market-adjusted return (stock − XBI) instead of the market model — over ~126 trading days a fitted alpha would compound into drift, and a 6-month pre-window would overlap the estimation window, so beta=1/alpha=0 is the robust long-horizon choice (deliberately a different estimator from the 30-day chart above). Reading it: Approved (green) is essentially flat for the first ~5 months and only lifts to about +5% in the final ~20-30 trading days — so the anticipation is a last-month effect, not a slow six-month drift. CRL (red) wanders up to ~+6% mid-window then back to zero before the one-day crash at T0 — no clean early-warning signal, just the surprise. Practical read: the tradeable Approved run-up window is roughly the last month before the decision, not earlier.

CAR over time

Same events plotted by catalyst date — shows both the outcome asymmetry and where Yahoo's coverage thins out (sparser before ~2013).

Long-window trend: T-1yr..T+1yr

Same method as the CTOD/EDGAR notebook's section 13 (Phase 3 Success price path): raw daily close indexed to 100 at the catalyst date, no benchmark adjustment — complements the tight CAR window above by showing the shape of the move, which a 5-day window can't. Line = median indexed price per week-offset; band = 25th-75th percentile across events. Caveat, same one that applies to the notebook's version of this chart: this is raw price, not sector-adjusted, so some of the drift is XBI/biotech-sector movement, not company-specific reaction.

Approved (n=209)
CRL (n=40)

Approved: 91.2 (T-1yr) → 100 (event) → 106.9 (T+1yr) — a gradual run-up into the decision, consistent with approval being anticipated rather than a surprise. CRL: 127.2 (T-1yr) → 100 (event) → 112.6 (T+1yr) — the stock was already declining over the year before a CRL (more so than after), then partially recovers over the following year.

Small-cap single-asset events: raw price

10 non-big-pharma events, each in raw dollar price on its own axis (small caps show a cleaner single-drug reaction than diversified big pharma). Black dashes = catalyst date; orange dots = other large daily moves (>12%), a proxy for earnings/other news since we don't have an exact earnings feed. The 5 CRL cases (EYPT, OCUL, HRTX, LPCN, TRVN) show the sharp drop at the event line; the orange dots confirm the event-day move is distinct from the stock's other volatility.

Small-cap single-asset events: indexed to 100 (combined)

All 10 small-cap events on one axis, each indexed to 100 at its catalyst date. Green = Approved, red = CRL. The CRL lines collapse at the dashed catalyst line; the Approved lines are comparatively muted around it (their move, if any, is spread out — consistent with the CAAR chart above).

Small-cap single-asset events: indexed to 100 (individual panels)

The same 10 events, each on its own indexed axis (base 100 at catalyst) so a single name's path is readable without the others overlapping. Black dashes = catalyst date; orange dots = other large daily moves (>12%, earnings/news proxy).

All priced events

Table view of the same 249 events.

TickerDrugStatusCatalyst dateCAR

Proposal: separating the full dataset into trial phases

The user's core question — the raw file's stage column mixes phase labels with regulatory-decision labels in one field, with no explicit phase-of-pivotal-trial link for Approved/CRL rows.

What the "Approved or CRL" column actually contains

It's not an outcome column and a phase column layered together by accident — it's a single catalyst-type field with 13 distinct values in the cleaned data:

ValueCountWhat it means
Phase 1 / 1b / 1-2259early readout, not yet pivotal
Phase 2 / 2a / 2b566mid-stage readout
Phase 2/323seamless/adaptive trial readout
Phase 3689pivotal-trial readout
NDA / BLA Filing, PDUFA10regulatory-process milestones, not trial results
Approved513terminal regulatory decision (positive)
CRL120terminal regulatory decision (negative)

Why Approved/CRL already is effectively "post-Phase-3"

FDA approval decisions are made almost entirely on pivotal Phase 3 data (accelerated-approval-off-Phase-2 is the exception, not the rule). So the Approved/CRL slice used above is already a reasonable proxy for "Phase 3 pivotal outcome" — this is the same population the old EDGAR-matched pipeline was trying to approximate at n=47, just reached here directly through a curated regulatory-decision date instead of guessing a trial-completion-to-filing match. That's the main reason this source is a real improvement, not just a different dataset.

Recommended linking scheme, to get an explicit phase per event

  1. Primary (free, no external calls): for every Approved/CRL row, look back within this same CSV for the most recent prior row sharing Ticker + a fuzzy-matched Drug Name (strip parentheticals/trial-name suffixes the same way the PubChem retry step did) whose status is Phase 1/2/3. That row's phase is the pivotal phase. Cheap, deterministic, no rate limits — the same trick that made PubChem name-matching recover 267 of 462 drugs here.
  2. Validation on a sample: cross-check ~30 linked pairs against Drugs@FDA (openFDA, free) by brand/generic name — it returns submission type (NDA/BLA) and approval history but not trial phase directly, so it validates the drug identity match, not the phase itself.
  3. Fallback for unlinked rows: ClinicalTrials.gov search by intervention name + phase=PHASE3 + status Completed, dated before the Approved/CRL catalyst date — same idea as the CTOD/EDGAR pipeline in the other project, but only needed for whatever step 1 can't resolve, which should be a minority.

What this unlocks beyond the Approved/CRL slice done here

The 1,547 Phase 1/1b/1-2/2/2a/2b/2-3/3 rows have no explicit success/failure label — only free-text Catalyst Description ("met its primary endpoint" vs. "did not meet…"). A simple keyword classifier on that text (same result-language approach used for EDGAR filing scoring in the other project) would let mid-phase readouts be priced the same way as Approved/CRL here — extending this analysis to Phase 1/2 catalysts, not just terminal regulatory decisions.

Source: BioPharmCatalyst scrape via GitHub (Tejas1415), pulled 2026-07-15. Pricing: yfinance daily closes, market-model CAR vs. XBI, T-2..T+2 window (same estimator as the CTOD/EDGAR project — short_window_car()). Small-molecule/peptide filter: PubChem MW ≤ 8000 Da on cleaned drug-name candidates. Code: scripts/25-28_biopharmcatalyst_*.py in hedgefund-analysis.
